observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the:

Municipal Treasurer to coordinate with the Provincial Treasurer to establish the respective shares of the Barangays out of the RPT collections by the Provincial Treasurer’s Office (PTO);

Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to transfer to the respective Barangays their RPT shares; and

Municipal Accountant to exert effort in retrieving data and information, conduct reconciliation of the Due to LGUs account with available records and make the necessary adjusting entries for deficiencies noted.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive shall: 

Require the focal persons/implementing office to expedite the implementation of the unimplemented projects, otherwise, re-assess and reprogram the funds to other workable projects; and

Periodically review the program/projects to be charged against the 20% DF to enable the LGU to address the development needs of the constituents.

We recommended and Management agreed to intensify efforts to enforce collection remedies provided by Republic Act No. 7160, particularly the sale of delinquent properties through public auction in order to demonstrate the Municipality’s will power and capability to enforce the laws on real property taxation and deter tax evasions.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the Municipal officials concerned to identify the land owned by the Municipality and undertake immediate action to expedite the titling of the land in the name of the Municipality where government properties are located so as to prevent the occurrence of disputes over the use of the properties in the future.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor refund the Transportation Allowance received for the period January to March 2019 amounting to ₱24,300.00.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor refund the Transportation Allowance received for the period January to December 2018 amounting to ₱97,200.00.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and SB Member refund the Transportation Allowance (TA) received for the period January to December 2017 amounting to ₱178,200.00.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Disbursing Officer to explain in writing the system of disbursement of the meal allowance for CDSA. Likewise, explain the difference/alteration of signatures in the payroll not acknowledged by payees with the actual signature during confirmation as confirmed by the audit team per attached schedule.

We also recommended the refund of the amount not received by recipients totaling ₱66,000.00 and excess cash advance amounting to ₱3,180.00 is required.
We recommended the refund of the total amount of PEI for CY 2015 totaling ₱770,000.00 paid to personnel.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the concerned Municipal Officials to refund the payment of Transportation Allowance (TA) amounting to ₱972,000.00.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Treasurer to strictly implement the pertinent rules and regulations on fund management particularly on the collection and deposit activities including the updating of recording in the cashbooks to deter the misappropriation of public funds.
We recommended that the LGU require the concerned employee-borrowers to immediately refund to the municipality the amount due them.
We recommended that the members of the BAC immediately refund the ₱25,000.00 representing BAC honoraria which was disallowed in audit.

We recommended and it was agreed that the LCE activate its Inventory Committee and direct the following:
The Inventory Committee:
To formulate a policy and guidelines on the conduct of its annual physical count;
To conduct a thorough physical count of all its properties and prepare and submit the RCPPE for all types of PPE; and
To use the appropriate forms and reports prescribed under the New Government Accounting System Manual for LGUs Volume 2.
The Municipal Accountant and the GSO Designate reconcile their respective PPE records.
We also recommended and it was agreed that:
The Accounting Department make the necessary adjustments as to the proper recognition of the Depreciation Expense of the PPE items and the donated vehicle; and
The GSO Designate to provide the Accounting Department with the supporting documents relative to the donated vehicle and in subsequent donations thereafter.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the following:
The Municipal Assessor to prioritize securing original or transfer certificates of title (OCT/TCT) for all land assets by expediting the requests/applications for land titles to the proper government agencies;
The reconciliation of the records between Accounting, Assessor, and GSO-Designate by verifying records and conducting a physical count to ensure the accuracy and completeness of the recorded Land account; and
Municipal Accountant to draw the necessary accounting entries to record the unaccounted lands and revalue the previously recorded lots for a more accurate valuation.

Since recovery efforts in this case are not feasible due to lack of supporting documents and the length of time that has passed since the incident, we recommended and it was agreed that the LCE review its financial policies and internal controls to prevent a recurrence. This may include enhancing oversight of financial transactions, conducting regular internal audits, and introducing more stringent checks and balances for individuals in financial roles.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following:
The LCE expedites the disposal of unserviceable properties by creating a Disposal Committee; and
The Municipality avails of the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts as provided under COA Circular No. 2020-006 to finally establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition, and accountability.

We recommended and it was agreed that the LCE direct the following:
The Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant with the necessary documents certifying the completion of the various LGU projects to support the preparation of journal entries and ensure timely reclassification of asset accounts; and
The Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary journal entries to record the completed projects and recognize the corresponding accumulated depreciation at year-end and thereafter ensure the timely reclassification of completed projects.

We recommended and it was agreed that the Municipal Accountant draw the necessary adjusting entry to derecognize the non-existent livestock and poultry recorded in the books.
We also recommended and it was agreed that the Office of the Municipal Agriculture notify the Accounting Department of any distribution of livestock or poultry made or death of the animals in their custody in their subsequent projects.

We recommended and it was agreed that the Municipal Accountant make the necessary adjusting entries to recognize the semi-expendable expenses on properties that have been issued in the prior years and during the Calendar Year 2024.

We recommended and it was agreed that the Management consider discontinuing its time deposit placement and instead utilize the funds in projects that would benefit its constituents.
We also recommended and the following were agreed upon:
The Acting Municipal Treasurer to coordinate with the concerned AGDB to verify the validity of the charges made, particularly the Documentary Stamp Taxes, and to require a recomputation of the time deposit amount upon maturity and discontinuance; and
The Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entries to appropriately recognize the relevant taxes and income in their appropriate period.

Based on the foregoing audit observations, we recommended and it was agreed that the LCE direct:
The HRMO:
To timely update the leave cards of each official and employee and provide the Municipal Accountant the necessary data i.e., the balance of unused leave credits at year-end for its recognition in the books of accounts (including leave credits earned, utilized, and year-end balances) for the computation of cost of employee benefits in preparation for the full implementation of COA Circular No. 2024-009 and its quarterly update thereafter;
To furnish every Department/Office the updated schedule of leave credits earned of officials and employees as often as practicable once records have been updated; and
To ensure that Sections 22 and 23 of the Omnibus Rules of Leave are followed before certifying in the Application for Leave on every request for commutation of leave credits.
The heads of Departments/Offices to include in their budget proposals for the Municipality#8217;s succeeding annual budget preparation the money value of outstanding leave credits and the cost of employee benefits which could be earned during the fiscal year.

We recommended and it was agreed that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to strictly comply with the procedures provided under Section 20, Volume I of the NGAS Manual for LGUs in the accounting of the Province#8217;s RPT share by preparing and submitting the summary of JEVs on a weekly basis, or at the most, monthly.

We recommended and it was agreed that the Local Chief Executive (LCE), together with the Municipal Planning and Development Officer (MPDO) and other responsible Officials and Employees:
Ensure that all programs, projects and activities funded under the 20% DF are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready; and
Revisit the programs, assess their feasibility, and take necessary measures to ensure the implementation of development projects identified in the AIP to maximize the utilization of the 20% DF.

We recommended and it was agreed that the LCE:
Immediately notify the Bureau of the Treasury of the bondable job-order personnel and apply for their respective fidelity bonding;
Require the market supervisor to assume direct responsibility and supervision over the market-based collectors; and
Instruct the personnel whose duties do not include issuance of cash tickets or collections of fees to refrain from performing such task to prevent unauthorized handling of government funds, or have them bonded if the Management intends to utilize them as collectors.

We recommended and the following was agreed in the LGU#8217;s subsequent implementation of its GAD PPAs:
Require the concerned Department Heads to notify the GAD Focal Person of any changes in the DILG-endorsed GPB and/or the need to implement additional PPAs relevant to current gender issues or GAD related undertaking; and
Ensure that the LCE formally inform the concerned DILG Office in writing regarding such changes.

While the remedies provided in Sections 256 to 260 of R.A. No. 7160 are temporarily put on hold due to the Tax Amnesty Program, we recommended and it was agreed that the LCE direct the Municipal Treasurer to conduct more aggressive tax campaigns in order to increase public awareness and eventually increase its tax collections through information dissemination by educating property owners about the importance of property taxes and the consequences of non-payment and transparency demonstration in the use of taxes which can build trust and encourage voluntary compliance.
We also recommended and it was agreed that the Assessor#8217;s Office reevaluate the properties claimed to be exempt from RPT and reclassify them appropriately.

We recommended and it was agreed that the LCE direct the concerned Department Heads to:
Review the status of implementation of various Programs/Projects/Activities (PPAs), coordinate with the identified source agencies, and submit the corresponding liquidation report for fully implemented PPAs to the concerned National Government Agencies to settle the LGU#8217;s obligations;
Sustain efforts to trace the status of unexpended fund balances that require further verification for their proper disposition; and
Ensure the immediate return or liquidation of the unexpended fund balances for completed projects to the respective source agencies that have already been identified.

We recommended and it was agreed that the Management take pre-emptive measures and inform all personnel such that the approval of loan applications will not cause their net take home pay to fall below the ₱5,000.00 required minimum.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to: 

Direct the Supply Officer and the Municipal Accountant to retrieve the documents relative to COVID-19 procured items;

Require the Supply Officer to prepare and submit the SSMI together with the RIS to the Municipal Accountant as basis of dropping from the books the supplies and materials issued to correct the overstatement of inventory accounts; and

Henceforth, require the periodic reconciliation by the Municipal Accountant and Supply Officer of their records relative to the procurement and issuances of supplies and materials to avoid misstatements of inventory accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to: 

Require the Municipal Treasurer to prepare and provide copy of the list of unreleased checks to the Municipal Accountant at the end of the year; and

Henceforth, direct the Municipal Accountant to restore to the Cash in Bank and corresponding Payable accounts the amount of unreleased checks at year-end.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:

Instruct the Municipal Accountant to notify the concerned accountable officers to liquidate their cash advances, otherwise, withholding of their salaries shall be made in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002;

Discontinue the granting of additional cash advances with the same purpose/s to the concerned Disbursing Officers, Special Disbursing Officers, employees and elected officials unless their previous cash advances are settled and proper accounting thereof are made; and

Henceforth, adhere strictly to the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the   LDRRM Officer to:

Assess the activities to be included in the LDRRMIP and determine if   the charges against the LDRRMF are appropriate; and

Ensure that the four DRRM thematic areas namely, disaster prevention/mitigation, preparedness, response and rehabilitation/ recovery activities and projects are prioritized.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive as the Head of the Procuring Entity direct the BAC to:

Conduct a thorough post-qualification and evaluation of bids to ensure that the winning bidder will be able to deliver the specific needs of the Municipality; and

Strictly comply with the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 9184 to ensure that all procurement awards and decisions will result in the most advantageous terms for the Municipality.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive as the Head of the Procuring Entity direct the Bids and Awards Committee to:

Carefully plan and monitor the procurement activities in the APP;

Ensure that the APP is in accordance with the AIP and the approved annual budget; and

Comply with the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184 in the implementation of the contract.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to: 

Direct the Municipal Accountant and concerned municipal officials and employees to submit the lacking documentary requirements of their travel claims, otherwise, cause the refund of the corresponding amount; and

Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant to ensure that travel claims are duly supported with complete documentation.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to refund the unutilized balances to the source agencies or the Bureau of Treasury; conduct reconciliation and retrieve data and information; and maintain subsidiary ledgers for each fund received from NGAs and monitor closely the transactions pertaining thereto.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the Municipal Treasurer to regularly furnish the Municipal Accountant a duly certified list showing the name of the taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year that shall be the basis of the latter to set-up RPT/SET Receivables at the beginning of the accounting period to conform with Sections 19 and 20, Volume I of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the LDRRM Officer to:

Ensure that the four DRRM thematic areas namely, disaster prevention/mitigation, preparedness, response, and rehabilitation/recovery activities and projects are prioritized.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Chairperson and Members of the BAC to refund the excessive price awarded for emergency purchase totaling to ₱122,850.00 and refrain from incurring the same offense in the future.

Require the Municipal Agriculturist to initiate the execution of a MOA with the Barangay Matag-ub Sitio Kinambud Farmers Association and Barangay Cabantog Farmers Association.

Require the Municipal Agriculturist/Project In-Charge to submit the documents pertaining to identification of beneficiaries and the Accomplishment Report of the ICFP- Pagkain ni Boss Project; and

Direct the Municipal Agriculturist/Project In-Charge to monitor the ICFP- Pagkain ni Boss Project and require the Recipients - Farmers Association to comply with their counterpart and other provision of the Project Proposal.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the: 

Municipal Accountant to maintain separate RAAOs for the current appropriations of the LDRRMF. The balance of the current appropriation of LDRRM Fund at the end of the year shall be transferred to the Special Trust Fund;

Municipal Accountant to set-up separate ledgers in the special trust fund for the transferred unutilized balance of DRRMF from the General Fund by year; and

MDRRMO to submit to the Audit Team a monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF duly prepared and certified by the Municipal Accountant reflecting the available funds, utilization and balances of the Special Trust Fund on a yearly basis.

Require the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and concerned Municipal Officials to ensure that the functionality of the developmental projects are maintained for the promotion of the general welfare of the constituents.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the SWM Board and MENRO to: 

Monitor the implementation and effectiveness of the ESWMP in order that measures to correct the deficiencies noted will be timely instituted; and

Revisit Republic Act No. 9003 and comply with the provisions of Sections 16 and Section 12 of the aforementioned law.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor and concerned officials agreed to:

Prepare an Organizational  Structure and Staffing Pattern Plan and formulate policies and guidelines so that the necessary and propriety of hiring job order employees are justified;

Incorporate in the job order contract the services to be rendered and the number of man hours, and quantity and duration of the works to be performed by the job order personnel and require them to submit quantified accomplishment report; and 

In coordination with the department heads, assess and review the necessity of hiring job orders by considering the existence of regular employees who could perform some of the contracted work. Maximize the services of the regular employees and minimize the hiring of job orders.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) to submit the Municipality’s GAD Plan and Budget to DILG and prepare the accomplishment report based on DILG endorsed GPB or LGU budget and submit the same to the Audit Team for verification.

1. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the HRMO to:


   a. Reduce the number of job hires especially  those  performing  clerical
      and administrative functions, and  with  duplicate  functions  in  one
      office. Hiring of workers should be made in a short  duration  and  as
      actual need arises and be deployed only to offices that  needed  their
      services the most.

   b. Review the profile of Job Hires, their  duties  and  responsibilities,
      actual assignment versus their accomplishment  and  cause  reshuffling
      when needed. Institute regular job rotation to place the right  person
      on the right job to deliver the services required.


   c. Require the rendition of overtime services by regular employees in the
      exigency of the service and to meet reports on  deadlines  instead  of
      hiring Job Hires.

   d. Adopt merit and incentive award strategies to motivate  all  personnel
      and to enhance their potentials in performing their functions.

  Treasurer not to issue cash tickets to  Job  Hires  since  accountability

  to enroll the Job Hires in the biometrics system so that their attendance
  will be substantiated. The  Department  Head  concerned  shall  sign  the
  electronic DTR of the Job Hire monthly under his direct supervision.

  issue Job Order contracts on a per office basis detailing the duties  and
  responsibilities  of  each  Job  Hire  duly  signed  by  the   respective

  individual Job  Hire.  The  Department  Head  concerned  shall  sign  the
  Accomplishment Report of  the  Job  Hire  under  his  direct  supervision
  detailing the actual accomplishment monthly.

  Heads to submit written justification for hiring job  hires  under  their
  offices for CY 2017.

  set forth in CSC MC No. 24, s. 2002  on  the hiring of  job  hires  and

  considered so  that  savings  derived  could  be  used  on  developmental

We further recommended that the Disposal Committee follow the disposal procedures and accomplish the forms to be used in reporting disposable supplies and property. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor program the construction of bodega for unserviceable properties and documents. We likewise recommended the disposal of unserviceable properties beyond repair and value less records according to the guidelines on Disposal.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to deposit all undeposited collections to the authorized depository bank and to strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 69 of PD 1445 and Section 32 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer reconcile the accounting and cashbook balances every end of the month. We further recommended that the Municipal Treasurer submit explanation on the difference between the cashbook balances and cash counted in January 10, 2017.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Agriculture Officer and the Job Hires to stop the practice of receiving collections for Egg Production (layered chicken) and turn-over to the Municipal Treasurer the collecting and disbursing function for proper recording and accounting of the Trust Fund. The remaining balance amounting to P39,320.92 shall be withdrawn from Janiuay Rural Bank and transferred to Land Bank Janiuay branch. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the submission of all documents to the OIC-Municipal Accountant relative to the collections and disbursements of the Egg Production (layered chicken).

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor require the immediate settlement of all cash advances after the purpose for which these were granted had been served or an accounting had already been made thereof pursuant to Section 89 of PD 1445 and COA Circular No. 97-002. We further recommend that additional cash advances should not be given to personnel unless their previous cash advances are first liquidated and/or settled. Also, effect suspension of salary of the concerned employee until they comply therewith. Under Section 3.3.2 of COA Circular 96-004, the Accountant shall: Send within ten (10) days before the expiration of the 30 days period specified under Section 16 of EO 248 and amended under EO 298, a written reminder under signature of the head of the agency or his duly authorized representative, enjoining the official or employee concerned to liquidate his travel cash advance. This is to preclude complaints arising from suspension of salaries due to non-liquidation of travel advances. Delete the name of the official or employee from the subsequent payroll until such time that the travel cash advance has been fully liquidated, if official or employee concerned fails to liquidate cash advance within the prescribed period. Verify the liquidation voucher and supporting documents and record the same in the books of accounts subject to the post-audit by the Auditor.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer require the Accountable Officers to maintain cash books for cash advances to facilitate reconciliation of their accountability with that of Accounting records as well as the cash on hand at any period of time.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and concerned municipal officials refrain from incurring additional PS expenses/cost until such time that the limitation is observed. The implementation of any increase in PS cost should be held in abeyance until the municipality shall not exceed its PS limitation/cap. We also recommended that all municipal officials and employees cause the refund of the CY 2016 Productivity Enhancement Incentive equivalent to P5,000.00 per employee or a total of P780,500.00 due to excess in personal services limitation as prescribed under Section 325 (a) of Republic Act No. 7160.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor refund the Transportation Allowance (TA) received for the period July to December 2016 amounting to P48,600.00. We further recommended that payments for Transportation Allowance should be based on laws, rules and regulations authorizing such payments to avoid suspension and disallowance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Local School Board discontinue the payment of electricity, telephone and other expenses charged against the Special Education Fund. Section 1 of RA 5447 on the prioritization of expenses chargeable to SEF must be strictly followed. We also recommended that the Local School Board prepare the budget for Special Education Fund in accordance with the rules and regulations of RA 5447 so that funds will be spent for the intended purpose as advanced in the regulation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the HRMO to: Reduce the number of job hires especially those performing clerical and administrative functions, and with duplicate functions in one office. Hiring of workers should be made as actual need arises and be deployed only to offices that needed their services the most. Review the profile of Job Hires, their duties and responsibilities, actual assignment versus their accomplishment and cause reshuffling when needed. Institute regular job rotation to place the right person on the right job to deliver the services required. Require the rendition of overtime services by regular employees in the exigency of the service and to meet reports on deadlines instead of hiring Job Hires. Adopt merit and incentive award strategies to motivate all personnel and to enhance their potentials in performing their functions. We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer not to issue cash tickets to Job Hires since accountability rests with bonded accountable officers. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the IT Technician to enroll the Job Hires in the biometrics system so that their attendance will be substantiated. The Department Head concerned shall sign the electronic DTR of the Job Hire monthly under his direct supervision. We likewise recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the HRMO to issue Job Order contracts on a per office basis detailing the duties and responsibilities of each Job Hire duly signed by the respective Department Head with justification on the need for hiring of services of Job Hires. The said contracts duly received/acknowledged by the individual Job Hire. The Department Head concerned shall sign the Accomplishment Report of the Job Hire under his direct supervision detailing the actual accomplishment monthly. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require all Department Heads to submit written justification for hiring job hires under their offices for CY 2017. We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor revisit the guidelines set forth in CSC MC No. 24, s. 2002 on the hiring of job hires and contractual personnel. Judicious use of government funds must be considered so that savings derived could be used on developmental projects most beneficial in uplifting the lives of its constituents.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit immediately the disbursement vouchers for post-audit so that review and evaluation could be effected and to avoid incurrence of suspension and/or disallowance. We likewise recommended that internal control be strengthened by ensuring that the concerned municipal employees strictly comply with the afore-cited implementing laws, rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the LDRRM Officer-Designate and other concerned officials to refund the amount of P46,000.00 representing advance payment to Supplier for 20 sacks of rice. We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant and LDRRM Officer-Designate prioritize the procurement of early warning systems, search, rescue and retrieval equipment and other equipage useful for disaster preparedness and disaster response other than administrative supplies and equipment. We also recommended that the LDRRM Officer-Designate see to it that charges to LDRRMF are in accordance with NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 and COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012. We likewise recommended that the LDRRM Officer-Designate submit the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment charged to LDRRM Fund which include the newly procured items. We recommended that the LDRRM Officer-Designate submit the LDRRM Plan for CY 2016 and thereafter and the monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF. Projects and activities charged against the LDRRMF shall be incorporated in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Plan (LDRRMP), and integrated in the approved Annual Investment Program (AIP) of the LGU.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor: 1. Require the Municipal Agriculturist to initiate the execution of a MOA with the Barangay Matag-ub Sitio Kinambud Farmers Association and Barangay Cabantog Farmers Association. 2. Require the Municipal Agriculturist/Project In-Charge to submit the documents pertaining to identification of beneficiaries and the Accomplishment Report of the ICFP- Pagkain ni Boss Project. 3. Direct the Municipal Agriculturist/Project In-Charge to monitor the ICFP- Pagkain ni Boss Project and require the Recipients - Farmers Association to comply with their counterpart and other provision of the Project Proposal.
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer immediately return or deposit the unspent balances of the PDAF/DAP funds to the Bureau of Treasury and to the Source Agencies for other fund transfers.

We recommended that the SWM Board: 1. Formulate and document systems, processes and procedures to serve as a guide by SWMO in the implementation of the ESWMP and its current SWM function. 2. Enact and enforce laws and/or ordinances related to the implementation of the activities under the plan including imposing penalties/sanctions to any violator. 3. Establish a risk management process to identify all possible risks in the implementation of SWM operations. The risks should be evaluated in order to determine the possible impact in the effective implementation of the ESWMP. 4. Develop appropriate action plans for the identified risks and determining the persons responsible in executing the plan at the appropriate time. The intended action should as much as possible, avoid the occurrence of the risk and/or manage its effect or impact in the attainment of SWM objective. There should also be periodic monitoring and review of the risk plan to ensure its relevance with the current trend in SWM operation. 5. Adopt revenue generating measures pursuant to Section 12 (4) of Republic Act 9003 in order to ensure the viability of its 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan. 6. Revisit the provisions of Republic Act 9003 dated January 26, 2001 and comply with the guidelines of Rule 13 Section 2 of the aforementioned law.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer indicate in the POW the detailed scope of work with the corresponding estimated labor cost, the target date to be started and completion, the specific location of the project and other important details required in the POW. We further recommended that the Municipal Engineer implement the priority projects of the municipality strictly in accordance with the POW and the detailed Bill of Materials and Cost Estimates. Any deviation thereof, shall be indicated in a Revised POW and corresponding Bill of Materials and Cost Estimates in order that actual implementation of the project could be monitored. We likewise recommended that the Municipal Engineer conduct regular monitoring of priority projects being implemented by the municipality and calculate accurately and precisely the materials to be used for each project. We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer require the Contractor to undertake the specific work not yet done otherwise, refund of the amounts equivalent to the works not applied for will be enforced. Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Engineer turn over to the General Service Office of the municipality the excess materials not applied for inventory and safekeeping and application to other projects by administration.
We recommended the refund of the total amount of P770,000.00 for CY 2015 PEI paid to personnel.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive refrain from assigning two personnel to handle the Petty Cash of the municipality to properly monitor its use for the intended purpose. We further recommend that only non-recurring, emergency and petty operating expenses will be charged against the fund and not the regular one. Likewise, adherence to Section 48 of MNGAS for LGU and Section 4.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 is hereby enjoined.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive establish specific guidelines in the granting of medical, burial and other assistance to its indigents so that funds intended for the purpose will be used effectively.

We recommended that the LGU GAD Focal Point System adhere to the provisions prescribed under the Philippine Commission on Women (PCW) DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Circular No. 2013-01 dated July 18, 2013 to ensure compliance in mainstreaming gender perspectives in local planning, programming, budgeting, project development, implementation, monitoring and evaluation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the concerned Municipal Officials to refund the payment of Transportation Allowance (TA) amounting to P972,000.00. We also recommended that payments for Transportation Allowance should be based on laws, rules and regulations authorizing such payments to avoid the suspension and disallowance of the same.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer to submit regularly the Monthly Reports of Fuel Consumption duly supported with Monthly Reports of Official Travels to the auditor for verification purposes so that the reasonableness of fuel and oils consumed during the period can be evaluated. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant discontinue the processing of claims for gasoline, oil and other lubricants unless these are supported with complete documentation.

We recommended that the Municipal DRRM Officer adhere strictly with the provision of Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and submit regularly to the COA Audit Team of the Municipality the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund on or before the 15th day after the end of each month through the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council and the Municipal Development Council.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the Bank Reconciliation Statements monthly and submit the same to the Auditor for verification pursuant to Section 74 of PD 1445. Timely preparation and verification of the financial reports will guide management in making sound decisions regarding the financial position of the Municipality. We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant enroll in the Internet Banking Facility for Institutional Clients of the Land Bank of the Philippines (weAccess) to secure updated bank statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the personnel in charge of payroll preparation to effect adjustments on deductions and inform the personnel concerned to make proper arrangements with the lending institutions on repayments of loans so as not to reduce the net take home pay below the required minimum balance set by law. We further recommended that the person in charge of the approval of loans verify the status of the net pay of the employee applying for such loan and not to approve those whose net pay is below the required amount of P3,000.00.
We recommend that the Municipal Treasurer see to it that payrolls and its supporting documents are conspicuously cancelled/invalidated as “PAID” after each payment to preclude double payment.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Treasurer to strictly implement the pertinent rules and regulations on fund management particularly on the collection and deposit activities including the updating of recording in the cashbooks to deter the misappropriation of public funds. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to secure documents relative to the two cases of misappropriations which could be used to support for the proper reclassification of the amount misappropriated for the fair presentation of the cash in-vault account in the financial statement.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer see to it that all disbursement vouchers are duly supported with complete documentations to ensure the propriety, validity and legality of the transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned persons liable for the payment of penalty to refund the total amount of P10,325.00 paid by the Municipality pursuant to Section 4.7 of Joint Circular No. 1-85. Thereafter, the Municipal Mayor should require the concerned personnel to strictly implement the provisions of Section 4.7 of Joint Circular No. 1-85 so as not to incur unnecessary expenses for the payment of penalty due to late renewal of registration of government vehicles.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer, as Acting General Services Officer, maintain and update Property Cards and Stock Cards to account for the receipt and disposition of the same and to facilitate reconciliation with the accounting records. The Municipal Accountant should likewise maintain the Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Card, Real Property Ledger Cards and Supplies Ledger Cards pursuant to Section 114 of the New Government Accounting System for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the LGU require the concerned employee-borrowers to immediately refund to the Municipality the amount due them. We also recommend that the Municipal Accountant should take up in the books of accounts the amount due from employee-borrowers with the following journal entry: Due from Officers and Employees- 395,310.96 Gov’t. Equity - P395,310.96

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure the titling of all parcels of land owned by the Municipality and prioritize those within the public market premises for the purpose of establishing ownership thereof and thus avoid future litigation.

The Local Chief Executive closely monitor all the activities of accountable officers especially the OIC-Municipal Treasurer. Local Chief Executive should make representation with the Office of the Bureau of Local Government Finance to appoint a qualified OIC-Municipal Treasurer.

We recommend that the agency strictly adhere with the provisions of Section 325 (a) of RA 7160 and Local Budget Circular No. 98 dated October 14, 2011 so as to avoid imbalance in the allocation of funds favoring personal services and as not to reduce the funds available for the delivery of basic services and development projects. We further recommend the refund of the total amount of PEI for CY 2015 totaling P770,000.00 paid to personnel.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive require the Local Finance Committee to advise the Local Sanggunian to observe the provisions of DILG-DBM, Joint Memorandum No.2011-1 on the use of 20% Development Fund, so that proper utilization and implementation of the developmental projects will be carried out effectively and efficiently.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive establish specific guidelines in the granting of medical, burial and other assistance to its indigents so that funds intended for the purpose will be used effectively.

Likewise, adherence to Section 48 of MNGAS for LGU and Section 4.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 is hereby enjoined.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor refrain from maintaining postpaid or prepaid mobile phone of more than one unit and to stop the practice of allowing personnel below the Division Chief rank to avail the same. We further recommend that the provisions of Sections 163 and 165 of GAAM, Vol. 1 and COA Circulars No. 77-55 and 85-55-A be strictly adhered to, to avoid incurrence of unnecessary expenses and disallowance in audit.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive create a Disposal Committee to take charge of the disposal of unserviceable properties and equipment. We also recommend that the said Committee will be guided by the procedures and guidelines on the disposal as well as the forms to be used in reporting thereof.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor instruct the concerned Municipal Officials to refund the payment of Transportation Allowance (TA) amounting to P972,000.00. We also recommend that payments for Transportation Allowance should be based on laws, rules and regulations authorizing such payments to avoid the suspension and disallowance of the same.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Engineer to submit regularly the Monthly Reports of Fuel Consumption duly supported with Monthly Reports of Official Travels to the auditor for verification purposes so that the reasonableness of fuel and oils consumed during the period can be evaluated. We also recommend that the Municipal Accountant discontinue the processing of claims for gasoline, oil and other lubricants unless these are supported with complete documentation.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant prepare the Bank Reconciliation Statements monthly and submit the same to the Auditor for verification pursuant to Section 74 of PD 1445. We further recommend that the Municipal Accountant enroll in the Internet Banking Facility for Institutional Clients of the Land Bank of the Philippines (weAccess) to secure updated bank statements.
We recommend that the Municipal Treasurer see to it that payrolls and its supporting documents are conspicuously cancelled/invalidated as “PAID” after each payment to preclude double payment.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive require the concerned personnel to register all motor vehicles of the Municipality in accordance with the provisions of Joint Circular No 1-85. Likewise, all vehicles owned by the Municipality should be stamped “For Official Use Only” as a deterrent for the possible misuse of the same.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive require the concerned persons liable for the payment of penalty to refund the total amount of P10,325.00 paid by the Municipality pursuant to Section 4.7 of Joint Circular No. 1-85. Thereafter, the Municipal Mayor should require the concerned personnel to strictly implement the provisions of Section 4.7 of Joint Circular No. 1-85 so as not to incur unnecessary expenses for the payment of penalty due to late renewal of registration of government vehicles.

We recommend that the Municipal Treasurer, as Acting General Services Officer, maintain and update Property Cards and Stock Cards to account for the receipt and disposition of the same and to facilitate reconciliation with the accounting records. The Municipal Accountant should likewise maintain the Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Card, Real Property Ledger Cards and Supplies Ledger Cards pursuant to Section 114 of the New Government Accounting System for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommend that Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Health Officer to refund the overpayment of Hazard pay amounting to P35,194.50 which exceeded the prescribed rates of 5% of their monthly basic salary for health workers with salary grade 20 and above pursuant to Section 21 of R.A. 7305. Henceforth, management should strictly comply with Section 21 of RA 7305 and its Revised IRR on the payments of Hazard Pay for health workers.

We recommend that the LGU require the concerned employee-borrowers to immediately refund to the Municipality the amount due them. We also recommend that the Municipal Accountant should take up in the books of accounts the amount due from employee-borrowers with the following journal entry: Due from Officers and Employees-395,310.96 Gov’t. Equity - P395,310.96

The Local Chief Executive ensure the titling of all parcels of land owned by the Municipality and prioritize those within the public market premises for the purpose of establishing ownership thereof and thus avoid future litigation.
